narrative.business_description: A.O. Smith Corp. is a leading manufacturer of residential and commercial water heaters, boilers, and water treatment products. Has two reportable segments: North America (76% of ’24 sales); and Rest of World (24%), which primarily serves China, Europe, and India. Brands include A.O. Smith, GSW, Lochinvar, Reliance, and Takagi. As of 12/31/24, had approx. 12,700 employees and 28 wholly-owned and leased manufacturing facilities. Smith Family Voting Trust owns 96.9% of Class A shares; Officers & directors, 1.7% of regular common; Vanguard Group, 11.6% (2/25 proxy). CEO: Stephen Shafer. Chair.: Kevin Wheeler. Incorporated: Delaware. Address: 11270 West Park Place, Milwaukee, WI 53224. Telephone: 414-359-4130. Internet: www.aosmith.com.

narrative.commentary:
A.O. Smith is bringing Leonard Valve Company (LVC) into the fold. Indeed, on November 12th, the maker of water heaters and boilers announced that it had agreed to acquire privately held LVC for approximately $412 million, consisting of $470 million in cash upfront, net of $58 million in related tax benefits that will accrue to the buyer. Founded way back in 1911, Rhode Island-based LVC has since become a leading provider of water temperature control systems, including digital and thermostatic valves. On an adjusted EBITDA basis, it was recently expected to earn nearly $35 million in 2026. No recent sales data is available.

At first glance, we like the move. Importantly, LVC will expand A.O.’s presence within the mechanical rooms that are the lifeblood of commercial buildings, thereby giving it a leg up on competitors with less product breadth. At the same time, LVC’s digital solutions ought to better position A.O. as a service provider than can offer real-time heating system monitoring.

Other changes may be forthcoming. To wit, as of late October, management was in the midst of a strategic review that could ultimately result in A.O. selling all or part of its operations in China. In 2024, the second-most populated country on the planet represented $760 million, or about 20%, of the company’s overall dollar-denominated sales. Amid continued weakness in China’s housing market, sales there were expected to decline 10% in 2025, following a local currency drop-off of 6% the previous year.

America’s large replacement market remains the big draw here. Within the U.S., the company typically generates 80%–85% of its overall water heater and boiler sales from existing home and commercial buildings, versus just 15%–20% from new construction. Importantly, the replacement and retrofit market represents a much larger sales opportunity and one that is more stable.

These shares no longer stand out for year-ahead price performance (Timeliness: 3, Average). Still, a corporate makeover through the acquisition of LVC and exit from China could be a catalyst.
narrative.footnotes.dividends_timing: Dividends historically paid mid-February, May, August & November; dividend reinvestment plan available.
narrative.footnotes.eps_adjustments: EPS excludes nonrecurring gain/(losses): ’10, ($0.47); ’12, $0.38; ’14, ($0.15); ’18, ($0.03); ’22, (1.60). Also excludes discontinued operations: ’10, $1.18; ’11, $4.18; ’12, ($0.08). Next earnings report due early February.
narrative.footnotes.shares_note: Common shares outstanding in millions, adjusted for stock splits.
